narrative_blocks.header: She signed her will with a mark
narrative_blocks.body: Margaret’s will of September 12, 1651, left legacies to her daughter Mary Bryan and to her sister’s children John and Abigail Bronson, naming son John Pantry sole executor — and she signed it “Margaritt X Pantry,” her mark in place of a name.

narrative_blocks.header: She signed her will with a mark
narrative_blocks.body: Margaret’s will of September 12, 1651, left legacies to her daughter Mary Bryan and to her sister’s children John and Abigail Bronson, naming son John Pantry sole executor — and she signed it “Margaritt X Pantry,” her mark in place of a name.

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Pantry (father per Founders of Hartford genealogy)

cross_connections.display_label: held the Cambridge lot adjoining Pantry's
William Pantry was in the Adventurers Party with John Talcott.
Adjoining home lots in the Hertfordshire Quarter, New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Home lot in Cambridge was between Rev. Thomas
narrative_blocks.body: Home lot in Cambridge was between Rev. Thomas Hooker's and James Olmstead's — Hooker's literal neighbor before the Hartford migration

narrative_blocks.header: Member of the "Adventurers Party" of 25 men
narrative_blocks.body: Member of the "Adventurers Party" of 25 men who explored the Hartford site in October 1635, led by John Steele — the same party that included John Talcott

narrative_blocks.header: One of sixteen founders living in Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: One of sixteen founders living in Hartford in 1635, before Hooker's party arrived in May 1636
Selectman 1641 and 1646; jury of the Particular Court 1639-1648; constable 1648/9

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter Mary Pantry married Richard Bryan, who…
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Mary Pantry married Richard Bryan, whose son Joseph Bryan married Mary Newton, a Hooker descendant

narrative_blocks.header: He went ahead of Hooker’s party
narrative_blocks.body: Pantry was one of the twenty-five-man “Adventurers Party” that John Steele led out to the Connecticut River in October 1635 — one of sixteen founders already living at Hartford when Rev. Hooker’s company arrived the next spring.

The Cambridge land records of 1635 place the carpenter’s home lot between Rev. Thomas Hooker’s and James Olmstead’s. He died one of Hartford’s wealthiest original proprietors, his estate at £1,011 10s.

parents.father_research_notes: Alexander Bryan (ca.1602 – between Jul 20 and Nov 6, 1679)

cross_connections.action: Confirm whether William Pantry of Hartford (father of Mary Pantry Bryan) is the same family as the Pantry name in HD0064
cross_connections.display_label: Richard Bryan's first wife Mary Pantry was daughter of William.

sources.notes: Primary source for Bryan family genealogy; includes Milford land plot map p.6

narrative_blocks.header: Bought Eaton's Neck from Governor's daughter, 1684
narrative_blocks.body: In 1684 he bought part of Huntington Long Island — known as Eaton's Neck — from the daughter of Governor Theophilus Eaton. He was at the time 'one of the richest merchants in Connecticut.'

narrative_blocks.header: Widow then married Connecticut Governor Robert Treat
narrative_blocks.body: After his death his third wife Elizabeth Hollingsworth Bryan married Governor Robert Treat of Connecticut as his second wife. The match joined two of the Colony's wealthy mercantile and political families through a widow-remarriage common to Puritan New England.

His sons Alexander, John, and Ebenezer settled on Long Island after his death. The 1907 Bryan family genealogy described their descendants there as 'now numerous' — a Connecticut merchant's bloodline becoming a Long Island maritime lineage.

bio.bio_blurb: Milford merchant of the colonial Bryan family

narrative_blocks.header: A Milford merchant gone before forty
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Bryan of Milford married Martha Whiting in 1683 and fathered the Bryan daughters of that town, including Susannah, who married Col. Roger Newton. He died around 1698, still a young man, leaving Martha to remarry the magistrate Samuel Eells.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Through his daughter Susannah's marriage to Col. Roger Newton, Samuel Bryan became grandfather to a branch of Thomas Hooker's descendants. His own father, Richard Bryan, tied the family to the prominent Bryan merchants of the New Haven Colony.
